And so a business owner wins by making the same project more valuable to his business than that of his company. So if you think about somebody who's trying to lose weight, all the different needs that they have, we want to make that person more valuable to our business than anything else. So by extension, the gyms that satisfy and monetize the most customer needs will dominate their respective marketplace, right? Like we've been able to grow in this space very quickly and it's because we've been able to make our customers more valuable and you do that by providing more value to them, right? And so the reason that the gyms will dominate is because the more ways we can monetize customer relationships and the more needs we can satisfy, the more money we can make, right? And then the more money you make, the more you can turn around and spend on marketing to attract more customers and then by extension, help more people. Sweet, so number one, people pay you twice as much money for supplements as they do for fitness services. Why would they do that? Because they value them more. So some of you guys may have heard this presentation. I gave this at phone hacking live. We got a lot of praise for this. And so it's just the concept of selling the vacation on the plane flight, right? And so you're selling Maui, you're not selling the pre-flight checklist, right? You're not selling the TSA, getting padded down, having some 18 year old, you know, pat you upside down, but their knuckles up the crotch. Like you're not selling the TSA, the airport lines packing to you, you're just taking your shoes off, a lot of the playovers connecting flights, person farting next to you, terrible smell, you know, turbulence baggage claim. And for us, within the context of fitness, we're not selling our modules, our meal plans, our macros, our workouts, our programming, our mobility work, the meal prep, all of that stuff, right? We're not selling that, because that sounds like work, right? We're selling Maui, right? We're selling the final destination. And in sales, you always sell the same thing, right? The difference is just the quality of the journey, right? Do they wanna swim to Maui? Because there's sharks and you could, you know, drown or get robbed. Do they wanna take a boat to Maui? It'll take a while and there's slow slash no wifi, you know, might burn up in the sun. Do they wanna take a normal flight to Maui? Okay, get there a little faster, still smells bad and has security, bad food, but they're gonna get there, right? Do they wanna take a private jet to Maui? Direct, nonstop, champagne on the plane, suitcase is packed on a narrow ride, right? Looking nice. The point is that everyone gets to Maui, everyone gets to the final destination, the variables are the speed and the quality of the journey. And the likelihood of arrival is assumed since it's sale, right? So let me show you how these variables work together in establishing value, all right? So this is like a really, really awesome concept. And I like hope you guys dig this because it's really cool. So anyways, so these are the four variables for value, all right? So you've got result, belief, result and belief divided by effort times delay, all right? That equals value. So I'm gonna pay this picture for you. Result is the end result meaningful, right? Like let's say someone's losing weight, that would be an end result that's meaningful. Belief, do they really think that they're going to achieve it? People sign up for things all the time that they don't think they're actually going to achieve? How much do they believe that they're going to achieve it? Effort, how much time stress sacrifice do they need to give up in order to achieve it? And then delay is how long will they have to endure that effort and cost pain before seeing the result, all right? When you put all four of these things together, you get value. So here's an example. Let's take meditation, right? So you see the column in the middle where it says result plus, belief minus, effort minus, delay minus, right? Result of meditation is chill out, feel happy, feel relaxed, decrease anxiety, right? That's a good thing. That's a powerful bonus. That's something that a lot of people want, right? So it's a plus. Belief, how many people think that they can meditate for 30 minutes and do so consistently? Maybe I'd start something or I'd start an online course on it or buy the app, but I'm like, I don't know if I'm actually going to do it. Maybe I'll give it a shot, right? So it can probably be a negative, right? Effort, how much effort am I going to have to put in? Well, I'm going to have to do 30 minutes every day and I'm probably not going to like, and I'm going to have a lot of suckage, you know, there's a lot of period of time where I'm not going to be good at it and I'm going to constantly be distracted and feel like I suck and not good at this. So there's been a lot of effort and I have to do it and stay still and not thinking about not thinking and all that stuff. And then the delay is that there's two types of delays. One is the immediate delay is it takes 30 minutes for me to like really get into, or maybe 15 minutes to really get into like my Zen state. But then there's the delay of like actually having the long-term benefits of meditating every day takes a while, right? And so in some ways, the value of this is like one out of four. It's not super valuable, which is why meditation is not a bazillion dollar industry, right? So let me give you a different example with the same end result, same exact end result. Xanax, all right? Big pharma, what's the result? Chilling, feeling relaxed, feeling, you know, well-being, et cetera, decreased anxiety. Belief that if I take this, I'm going to feel that way. Yeah, I believe that if I take it, I'm going to feel that way. Effort, I have to swallow a pill. Delay, 15 minutes, right? Which one has more value? Xanax, which is why Xanax is a bazillion dollar, you know, like anti-anxiety pills or any billion dollar industry, right? That's why meditation isn't. It's a difference in value, right? So why am I saying this? Let me give you an example that you guys might be more familiar with that we're all familiar with. So we got fitness, we've got the big, big result, which is the only thing we have going for us. We've got life transformation, energy, confidence, you know, looking good, feeling good, all of that, right? That's our big belief, that's our big result. But when people sign up at the gym, what's the likelihood that they actually think they're going to achieve it? Whether you convince them or not, you convince them to buy the service. A lot of them still don't believe they're actually going to hit what they, what you were saying they're going to hit, they're like, you know, I'd be happy a third of that came true, right? The belief isn't there most times. Effort, what are we, what are they going to have to give up? They got to give up all the foods they love. They got to wake up early, they got to go to bed later, or they got to go to bed earlier. They have to explain to this to their husband, they got to cook two different types of things. Like they have to give up everything that they love in life in order to have this, right? It sucks. Huge amount of cost, they don't believe it's going to happen. And then what's the delay? Even if they do this, they're sore, they wake up, they have to sweat, they feel nauseous. They're like, they have to, you know, cook two different types of meals for their kids and for their husband versus them. They got all this stuff that they have to do this cost. They still don't even see the result four months if not years, right? Which is why fitness is actually really hard to sell. Which is why when people buy fitness or they buy personal training, you have to sit down with them for 45 minutes to explain to them why it's so important and why it's so valuable to them. But like people happily go right into a medispa and drop a couple of grand. Because the value is different. Which, as a side note, is why I believe the bestest people in the world actually started fitness. Because we have learned to sell something that is low value. It's perceived as low value. Which is why it's a hard sale, right? Now, let's look at supplements through the value equation lens. Supplements, what's the big result? It's actually the exact same result. You're selling the same Maui as you do with training. They're going to get there faster, right? The result is that they're gonna lose weight. The result is that they're gonna feel better. They're gonna feel better during the workouts. They're gonna sleep better. They're going to lose fat at a faster rate over an extended period of time. All these things are results that are just as aligned with services, right? So it's a plus, same plus. Belief, what's the belief that they're going to use, let's say a pre-workout and actually feel more energized when they work out? Pretty high, right? Okay, what's the amount of effort that they need to put in? Shake it up, drink it, or put a pill in, drink it, right? What's the delay? This one's a half score because let's say, it depends on the product, but let's say something for like, like intra-workout, right? So branched human waste, everyone knows that's gonna, it's gonna increase the amount of lean body mass they're gonna have over an extended period of time, right? Something like that, like an intra-workout product, they can feel that, like the beta alanine, the tingles, they can feel that stuff immediately. So there is an immediate feeling that they get, but it does take the same amount of time to reach their fitness goals as it does with service, hopefully, like from a reasonable amount, like they're not gonna notice that they gained 10% more muscle in 12 weeks. They will have, they just won't necessarily realize that, right? And so that's why the value score for supplements versus training is three and a half out of four versus one out of four, right? It's perceived as more valuable, which means, yes, they do perceive supplements as more valuable, right? Now, here's some crazy stats for you. Supplements are twice the size of the industry as the gym industry. So there's $27 billion a year that come in from membership sales, right, for gyms. There's $52 billion a year that come in from supplement sales, right? It is twice the value, which means that on average, customers spend twice as much money on supplements as they do on service. Twice. Oops, good back one. So let me give you a real world example. Consider what other products and services are sold within the larger context of the weight loss industry as a whole, right? And just like their results and their costs, just like, let's zoom out for a second. So this is a weight loss clinic. Some of you guys have heard of these like cool sculpting, things like that, right? So this is the number one most used cool sculpting before and after. Some of you guys have probably seen it, especially if you're a lady, you've probably seen these ads. These are run as TV ads. These are run as magazine ads. They're all over the place, right? Now, if you look at these pictures, right? And you can even see it says before and then it says 12 weeks after and then 12 weeks after. So if you're looking quickly, which is what most people do, you see before and you look at the end, it says 12 weeks after, but it's really 24 weeks because they're sneaky, right? But who here thinks that in 24 weeks, they could achieve a more significant result for a client than they see here. Most of us, right? Most people feel that way. Now, shouldn't it shock you that this lady probably spent between $2,000 and $3,500 for this result? And being real, she probably also changed her diet, but like we're just did a cleanse before she did it. She did her final. Anyways, I want to give you that point is. So the point is, is that she's spending $2,000 to $3,500 for this transformation. And yet we struggle to get people to buy a $150 a month membership. Why? Because they don't value it as much because it costs more, not just in money, but in time and effort. This lady only had to come in for an hour twice. And then here she is, she has a before and after. So she values the fact that she's going to get a flat tummy for only two hours of work, right? And nothing else, doesn't have to change your diet, doesn't have to just have to work out, doesn't have to be sour, doesn't have to wake up early, doesn't have to have nothing, right? And that's the difference. That is why they get to charge $2,000 to $3,500. And we are struggling to get $150 a month, right? And so prices like this may shock you, but they shouldn't because these are actually not really rich people who are doing this. I'm telling you, I promise you this, okay? These are regular people paying premium prices for things that they value. So let me give you a even more tactical example. So this is from a data I got from Chris Colley, who was the original franchiser or a thinnick. They had 220 franchise locations and a weight loss franchise, okay? So the exact same industry as us, brick and mortar service, they just sold supplements as their primary means of revenue and they gave their service away for free. Yes. So what would happen is people would come in for a $5 a week or $30 for 30 day promo. So your typical LBO, all right? And when they'd come in, they would give them that $5 a week, $30, 30 day promo for the service of helping them lose weight. But there was no gym, there was no workout, it was nutrition coaching, right? So then they're upsold in that first visit of 599 supplement starter kit. And a one free year of service. Just as a total side note, I love seeing like cross industries when the price points are around the same. Like we have all kind of found that 599 is the, is that I think the sweet spot for general populations coming in for people who don't need to be sales rock stars can still close that sale. They found the same number too. All right, so just a side note for you guys. But five days after selling that starter kit, they would come back in and sell them between $1510,000 for the supplements. Yep. This is her franchise. I talked to her all about her numbers all day long. This is how they did it. How could they do that? Because people value supplements more, right? And these are normal people, these aren't rich folks. They're coming in for $5 promos, $30, 30 days, they're not ballin', right? And fun fact, their most popular and mandatory supplement was a fat burner that they sell for $150 a bottle and it total cost them less than 20 bucks to manufacture. And these were doctors and nurses who gave their time away for free. Doctors and nurses were the ones who wrote the programs, right, for them to do. So why would a doctor or a nurse give their time away for free? Because they realized the truth, which is that the average customer values tangible goods over intangible products and services. And that's the average customer. It's worth noting that everyone here on this call, right, the wealthiest people reverse this because they value their time and they invest in coaching and information, which is why you guys are here, right? The wealthiest people in the world value intangible products more. But back to why average consumers prefer tangible goods. People have this weird belief, and I don't know if you guys have ever experienced this, but I remember when I would sell it, it came up all the time, is that they had this weird belief that I shouldn't get paid for services, right? Because in their minds, they don't get paid that much for their time and you don't have more education than they do, so why should you? You're no better than them. And they start doing this back in napkin math. They're like, if I buy 10 sessions for $600, that means he's making $6 an hour, and I don't make that, so why should he? He's not smarter than I am, blah, blah, blah. And they start telling the story, right? But if they spend their money on products, right, they believe that the more they pay, the higher quality the product is, which is true in a lot of cases, right? But we need to take advantage of this fact. Like the doctors did. And we can realize that we can make more money selling people what they want to buy. Like sometimes when people buy services that are more expensive than what they charge for their time, it makes them feel bad about themselves because they could never sell their time for that much. And we're talking general population. But when they buy product, they don't feel that way. They feel good because now they feel like even acid or consumable, that's more valuable to them, right? So here's an example that came up so many times, I just used Rosie as a figurehead here and I was reminded by someone that this is actually a guy in the picture, to just go with it. Rosie is that upset that she looks like a man in her picture, not that looking like a man is bad. You know what, I'm just, this claim was a side HR complaints. So she comes in and says, you know, I can't afford this, you know, challenge program, whatever, I need a payment plan. And Rosie was actually somebody who came in and she did $99 a week and she was like, I'm a, she clean house. She's like, I'm a business owner, you're a business owner. Like, let's see if we can kind of deal here. You know, like, well, I don't think, dude, please stop grinding me. And so finally she said to you, pay me $99 a week, okay? Two days later, she comes in and says, and I make the nutrition, you know, orientation, I say, these are the products you need to buy. And she's like, yes, I'll take all of them, there's anything else that I need, okay? She dropped $600 on supplements two days later. $600, two days later, and two days prior, she couldn't afford $600. She needed weekly payments for six weeks because she does not value my time. Straight up, didn't value service. And like, I can either try and change her beliefs about humanity or I can just sell her what she wants to buy. Right? So the key point here is that supplements will make you more money because customers perceive them as more valuable, right? And on top of being perceived as more value, they stack on top of existing services. So supplements actually represent entirely different category of purchasing decisions. So it's not like either or fitness and, you know, all of the services and product, it's both. And so I use this story and everyone always laughs at it because they always remember it. So, you know, maybe that's why I keep telling it, but consumer buying pockets are a lot like how my grandmother used to force feed me. So fun fact for everyone, don't live in the same apartment building as your grandmother. I was told that I should do this. When I was right out of college, I got an apartment in the same building as she did. The fun secret, you can never actually see your grandmother or call her enough when you live in the same building to make her happy. So I didn't even speak the same language as her. So like they're not even productive conversations anyway. She would just want to hear me breathe on the phone. Anyways, so she would always, you know, prepare these huge dinners for me. And so like when I, when she, my grandmother would make a dinner. I mean, there would be like 10 to 12 full family, like size bowls of rice, bowls of chicken, bowls of watermelon slices, you know what I mean? Just crazy amounts of food. And it would literally only be me. And I would skip my breakfast and my lunch and I'd go there almost like nervous about what I was going to have to encounter. And I'd be eating and I'd be sweating and I have to wear elastic pants because like I knew what I was getting into. And she would, and always she would just like, she'd be like more, more. And I was like, no, like I'm freaking done. And then, and then after I've been stuffed with foods, you know, brow sweating, she would come and just bring dessert, right? And of course I'd always have another slice of pie and some ice cream. And anyways, this, this, this continued, you know, this torture continued for a long time. I'm kidding. She's a lovely lady. But one time I came with my dad. So my dad's witnessing this, right? And so I just finished the first course of just all of the food, food, right? And then she's clear in the plate and she whispered something under her breath and farsi, which I don't speak that well. And he just cracks up. He loses it. And I was like, what is she saying? Like, what's, is she purposely torturing me? Like, I need to know, like if there's intention behind this. And he was like, she said that you filled up your regular stomach, but your dessert stomach's still empty. And so he just cracked up because that's what she was saying. And that's what she lived by. And the thing is, is that like consumer spending habits are a lot like that, right? Like I would still always have the slice of pie, the slice, you know, the scoop of ice cream after being full. And the same thing happens with how they spend money. And so you might have maxed out someone's budget on training services, but their supplement compartment is entirely untapped. And so it's not like they have X amount of discretionary. It's like, this is how much I spent on groceries. This is how much I spent on training services. This is how much I spent on supplements, vitamins, like there are different categories. And the more wallets we can get into, the more needs we can solve, the more money we can ultimately make, right? And so it works a lot like the dessert stomach. And so yeah, it means that fitness and supplements are additional instead of either or. And so to recap, you'll make more money from selling supplements because consumers spend twice as much on supplements as they do versus service. They're easier to sell. Consumers will pay you more for it than they will for fitness. They stack on top of what you're already selling. And so now that we covered that, the next natural question is like, all right, I get it. I'll make more money with them. But how does this actually help my customers, right? Like, I don't want to feel like I'm selling out, et cetera. So that's why the next secret is why not selling supplements is doing a massive disservice to your members. So one of the stories that I tell us while I kind of illustrate this is the ham and the garlic. I'll make it super short. There's a little boy and his grandmother and he's got his dog and it's got ticks on, right? And so he's upset because he's thinking like, my dad gave me this dog to take care of and he's worried because he's like, what does it mean? I'm not taking care of my dog. My dad's not proud of me. He's super, he's got lots of his arguments. So he goes to his grandmother and he's like, please help me, how do I get these ticks off the dog? And she says, me, well, like give him some garlic. He'll eat it, he'll sweat it off and then the ticks will go away. So the little boy goes over, tries to give the dog garlic, the dog's like, I want that stuff, it smells terrible. She goes back to grandmother. She's like, oh, me, well, did you try and give it to him? He's like, yeah, you told me to give it to him. Oh, you gotta wrap it in some ham and then give it to the dog. So the boy wraps it in ham, gives the dog, dog eats it right up. Two hours later, starts sweating out garlic ticks, you know, ticks go away. And so the moral of the story is that a lot, like we are a lot like the dog. And now we need to give people what they want in order to give them what they need. A lot of people don't know what they need or don't want what they need, but we have to give them, we have to wrap, like the Trojan horse, like we gotta wrap what they need with what they want. All right? So there it is, there's the moral. You gotta give people what they want to give them what they need. So here's the four stories that really changed. It's when you're the most excited, when you start a program that you should be doing that, and that you want to do that, you want to spend the money. Why are we not giving them the opportunity? Right, which leads me to the fourth experience of this, the last one. It's just a tale of two clients. So this is Jamie and Jennifer. And these are two prototypical clients. Jamie's the lady who comes in and, you know, I was like, listen, this is what you need to do. And she was like, okay, I'll trust you. I'm going to do it. This is a big investment, but like, I'm going to follow through, right? And so she will always listen to instructions. And she, you know, she invested more money than she had at the time. And she was like, but I believe that like, I really need to do this, right? Jennifer on the other hand was constantly asking, was like, I don't know if I want to do that. Like, blah, blah, blah, like always fighting me on every single point, right? And didn't want it. She was like, I don't want to invest that kind of money. It's not worth it to me, blah, blah, blah, blah, right? Now, fast forward, right? This is a note that I've learned over and over and over again. The more they pay, the more they pay attention. And you guys have heard me say like, we vote with our dollars about the things that we care about. So the goal here is to try and get people to vote more times, to care more about this thing that we're trying to get them to do is to change the behavior, which is really hard. So the higher the investment we can get, the more they'll pay attention to it, which as a coach is kind of something that we should be using to our advantage, right? Like we should use every tool in our advantage to increase buy-in so that we can have higher average successes across the board, right? And I always have this note for the single mom challenge. I don't know if you guys have ever done this. I sponsored so many single moms and just single parents during the program. And I told every one of them, I was like, I have sponsored a lot of people. I've never had anyone finish. And it was true because they never put anything down. They never followed through. And so just a fun one for you guys. This actually is Jamie. Jamie was a single mom of four and she drove Uber on the weekends in order to pay for extra product and for the membership stuff. So like, if we can get our clients more invested, doesn't matter what their situation is, like they're going to get better results. She lost like 75 pounds in 12 weeks and like changed her life. And I took these supplements, like I take supplements, a lot of you too take supplements. How could I rob them of the choice at the very least, right? And still to this day, it still gets me excited and why would I not let my clients experience the same feeling, right? And so naturally at this point, that's where everything, I was like, I'm going to sell supplements. I'm going to go all in on this and I'm going to learn it and I'm going to do it. And that's where everything became hard and I made a fool out of myself and lost a lot of money. Which leaves me to secret number three. How to sell supplements without price competition risk or dollars out of pocket? So I was excited to sell something that I ran into a few problems, right? The first one was the money down problem. So as soon as I was like, great, I'm going to go buy some supplements so I can sell them. They were like, cool, well, we sell, we send pallets over. So pallets should be expensive, which means the minimum order has to be like $3,000 or $4,000, whatever it was, depends on the supplier. And what's crazy is that every time I would do this, I would always feel the money go out, but I would never really feel the money come back in. We'd always like trickle back in, but it was always like, man, I'm going to have to pony up. I'm going to have to pony up and then drop another 4K or another 5K, right? And so it's just like, I always had to put money down and like have cash sitting on my shelves, getting dusty, right? The second problem was the impossible favor problem, which is no matter how many times I tracked who's ordering what, who's getting what flavors, who is getting what products, I could never get it right. So it's like, if you've bought t-shirts for 100 people, if you're trying to get the size as you will literally never succeed and I would always end up like 16 banana creams. Like there's just nothing I can do about it, right? Which led to the third problem. So I couldn't make these small orders because when I sit down with the money down problem, I couldn't make tiny orders because there was fixed shipping. So I had to make minimum orders that were X amount. And so I was forced to wait until I sold out of almost everything before I was able to re-up. And so this made my retail look terrible, which then further compounded the problem and slowed down my sales. It compounded the selection problem because like I wouldn't have like good selection as well. So it didn't look good and I also didn't have selection. So people couldn't even get the thing they wanted. And then it made me even more fearful the next purchase would get the another cash out. Which leads to the fourth problem, the Amazon problem. And this has started to happen when I was selling them is that like there wasn't a lot of margin and like the margins are rotted, right? So there wasn't a lot left and every time I tried to raise my prices, people would come back, pissed off because they'd find something on Amazon, right? The second one is if I did raise my prices, magically, they'd look on their phone and they'd match it for less and then they'd say, no thanks. There was one time we lost an entire room with 28 people because somebody was like, I found it for $7 cheaper on Amazon. And I literally lost the entire room and like I felt horrible. I was like, I really never want to do this again. But then I got back on the horse because that's what you got to do and sold the next person. But it still felt horrible and I still remember it. The third thing is the second sale loss. So if I did somehow magically sell someone, they would be like, thanks. And then they would go to Amazon and buy everyone after the first one from there. Fun fact, I was mutant proteins, number one facility in the nation and still never got paid past the first sale. I had a girl come back two years later and I happened to be at each be at this time. So this was like a coincident of coincidences. I was just like at the front desk and she's like, is Alex here? I was like, hey, what's going on? And she was like, you may not remember me. I was a client a while ago. Like, I just, I moved. I just want to dip in and say hi. I was like, hey, what's up? And she's like, you know what's funny? I still buy that protein that you told me to get. And I was like, great. Like I'm really glad that mutant still makes money off of the recommendation that I made for you two years ago. Like glad I made my first $10 on that. Thank you, Susan. She probably wasn't named Susan. But like I only made the first sale and every sale after that, the company or Amazon would get. Right? And the last problem that Amazon provided was like the proprietary problem, which is that sometimes they'd simply find a product that just had some of the ingredients in it and just compare labels and buy that one instead. Right? Which would mean that wouldn't buy the one that I wanted. The fifth problem was the crap in a bottle problem. So the product like that I had was crappy and I did know that at the time, but I felt like I had no choice but to buy cheap stuff and sell it for a lot to at least maximize the first transaction. I was like, listen, I get it. Like I'm not going to make recurring on this. I'm not going to make anything fast. The first one I was like, but I might as well maximize this first transaction, at least I can liquidate my acquisition costs. Right? And I don't say it was the best decision in the world, but like I'm just being honest with you guys. Like that's what I did. Problem six was the commodity problem. So no matter what I tried, I was always the bit of man trying to get a piece of the pie and the customer wanted to cut me out. Manufacturer would cut me out. Everybody wanted to cut me out. Right? The sixth problem was the Mr. Money Bag problem, which is that I always wanted to sell high ticket sub-insiders, but I was never able to because it would empty me out. So like I would, if I were to do it and have some Mr. Money Bag roll up in his rolls, right? And I'd be like, Hey man, you should buy 12 months worth of product. You would literally clean everything I had out. And then the rest of my console, so they wouldn't be able to sell anything for. And so it was like, I would, the best customers would essentially leave undersold, which is hard grade. And problem eight is that, I call the unhappy trainers problem is that they always wanted to sell stuff they were quote sponsored by, right? Like, you know, they're getting, they're getting T-shirts from Nutrishop, but they're getting 5% off, you know, the Nutrishop products and they're like, I want to sell that stuff instead. Even though they make anything, right? And then I was always somehow wrong in their commission, so I don't know why, but it always ended up working that way. And then finally was the tech about the problem. So that I had no idea how to sell these things, right? And whenever I talk about hyper-severely body master, blah, blah, blah, blah, everyone's eyes would glaze over. Right? And so the old way was buy a lot of product, front type cash inventory, run out of popular flavors, never gets the right amounts. No margin left over with Amazon, tons of price competition. I've never made a sale past the first sale. My retail looked like shit. I had crappy product. I was seen as a blood-sucking gentleman. I had no way to sell high-tech and front-type packages, no trainer commission system, sponsorships or certifications to get buy-in from my actual training staff, and then I never knew how to sell the product. So despite having my gems and doing wall-wass up on sales, I always had to work around these issues, right? It wasn't fun, it was a pain. Until now, which is the new way. Anyone want to know what the new way is? Yes, yes. Rocky, okay, sir. So this is my dream supplement coming. So this is what I set out two years ago. This was like, this is what I wrote down. I was like, this is what I want. I wanted zero inventory costs. I wanted recurring revenue with auto-ship. I wanted to keep getting paid if they kept buying stuff, even if it wasn't for me. Like that girl who came back, I was like, I should have been paid for that entire two-year period. I wanted the option to sell in bulk up front. I wanted price protection and premium positioning. I wanted celebrity endorsement, celebrity. I wanted proprietary blends and non-generic names to further protect for price comparison. I wanted as much margin as possible. I wanted endless supply and choices. And I only wanted to sell high margins. I was not messing around with low margin products, which waste consumer dollars and give them to someone else instead of me. And since it didn't exist, we decided to make it. And so this is the new retail model that will put all others out of business and reclaim the business lost in the GNCs and vitamin chops of the world. So I told you guys we wanted zero inventory costs. So we created a drop-shipping model. So we pay for the cost of storing inventory. That's our actual warehouse. Check it out. We wanted recurring revenue with auto ships. We created auto ship cash flows. So everyone can go and subscribe and save for any products they have. I wanted to keep getting paid if they kept buying stuff. So we have sticky commissions so you can get paid now and then they keep buying one year later and then you get paid later. Even if it's like someone stops buying and then buys a year later, you still get paid. I wanted the option to sell in bulk up front. So I'd have three, six and 12-minute packages, which is $300 to $5,000 sales. All of this stuff is available. You can make a $5,000 sale now. You don't have to carry the inventory and you can really get paid on it. Now, when it trainer buying, so we had commissions tracking trainer certifications and sponsorship programs that we do so that we can increase trainer buying. When it price protection and premium positioning. So there's actually listings on two different sites. The main site is actually listed 40% higher than the back door distributor site and there's centralized pricing control. So it means that like we control the pricing and the pricing doesn't go down. So what you see here at the bottom is like, what they're gonna see is $97, $97, $107, but what you will sell it for would be like 67, 67, right? And so they're going to be getting a deal and they will never be able to shop you. I want a celebrity endorsement celebrity. So I had PhDs, Olympians and pros actually endorse it, right? And on everybody you see here is PhD formulated trustable by Olympians. I want a proprietary blends and non-generic names to further protect the price comparison. So all blends are proprietary, but with the expensive ingredients pulled out for quality comparison. Meaning if like creatine monohydrate, for example, would be something that is a cheap product or cheap ingredient. Caffeine is a cheap ingredient. Well, we don't have caffeine in products. But I'm just saying they're cheap ingredients. So cheap ingredients we put inside a blend so that we can say, hey customer, like I want you to have the exact amount that's in this blend, because we know that it's been put in these right doses to exhibit X result. For the expensive ingredients, we pulled all those out so that you could say, listen, there's nothing that has this amount of this ingredient in it. You could shop around for it. And if they do, they'll notice that full bottles of just that single ingredient are more expensive than the entire bottle, right? And so it maximizes your ability to sell it on both sides.
